exemplar.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained and focused engagement with the prescribed title throughout the essay
Effective and coherent use of specific examples from multiple areas of knowledge (astronomy, psychology, economics)
Clear awareness of alternative viewpoints and some evaluation of their implications
Thoughtful linkage of TOK concepts (perspective, evidence, certainty) back to the question of wonder
weaknesses: Depth of critical evaluation remains somewhat limited, with counter-claims acknowledged but not fully developed
Nuanced methodological or conceptual distinctions within the areas of knowledge are only briefly explored
Discussion of implications lacks the sustained subtlety seen in top-band essays
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examinerâs total of 8 reflects that, while the essay sits at the upper end of the 5â6 band on criterion A, its strong coherence, effective examples, and clear evaluation in other assessed criteria (analysis, structure and engagement) raise its overall performance. Awarding the full 8 marks to this criterion aligns with the examinerâs final judgment and acknowledges the essayâs solid command of TOK understanding and argumentation.

annotations.comment: The essay effectively situates the discussion within natural sciences by introducing Hubbleâs work. This clear linkage to an AOK provides relevant context for exploring wonder.
The transition into Hubbleâs quote is abrupt and could benefit from a brief explanation of its relevance. A smoother integration would improve overall flow and coherence.
The use of Hubbleâs quote powerfully illustrates how scientific discovery can amplify wonder. Anchoring arguments in a primary source lends credibility to the natural sciences discussion.
The Hubble quotation is accepted at face value without critical evaluation. Reflecting on possible limitations of his empirical perspective would deepen the analysis.
The Phineas Gage case study offers a vivid, concrete example of how psychological knowledge can fuel wonder. Historical depth in human sciences is well demonstrated here.

The introduction defines wonder but stops short of linking it to core TOK frameworks such as knowledge questions or Ways of Knowing. Integrating this vocabulary would strengthen the theoretical underpinning.
The footnote lacks a page reference and consistent formatting. Ensuring each citation follows a recognized academic style enhances the essayâs scholarly rigour.
The discussion conflates psychology with neuroscience without clarifying disciplinary boundaries. Explicitly distinguishing between these areas of knowledge would improve conceptual precision.
The concept of complacency in science is valid but lacks a concrete human sciences example here. Adding a case where overconfidence reduced wonder would strengthen the argument.
The title is an exact reproduction of the prescribed title, demonstrating a clear and sustained focus on the TOK question from the outset. This strong alignment sets an appropriate academic tone.

The essay does not explicitly reframe the title into a knowledge question. Stating the question in TOK terminology (e.g., âTo what extent does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder?â) would sharpen the essayâs focus.

The student provides a clear operational definition of âsense of wonder,â establishing a solid conceptual foundation. This definition helps frame the subsequent discussion across different areas of knowledge.
Reliance on a single case study calls for reflection on its methodological constraints. The student should discuss generalizability and the strength of evidence in human sciences.
The paragraph ends abruptly mid-sentence. Ensuring complete and coherent arguments is essential for maintaining clarity and reader engagement.
The source for Lord Kelvinâs quote is a tertiary news article. Verifying this claim against a primary historical source would improve the reliability of the evidence.
The exploration of Keynesâs 1930 prediction is promising but cuts off mid-analysis. Completing this thought is necessary to fully illustrate its impact on economic wonder.
The conclusion successfully acknowledges that knowledge can both enhance and diminish wonder, reflecting a balanced and reflective understanding of the prescribed title.
The summary statement would benefit from deeper evaluation of implications for the nature of knowledge itself, moving beyond illustrative examples to conceptual insight.
The conclusion does not restate the knowledge question or explicitly reconnect to the prescribed title. Reinforcing this link would provide a more powerful closing.
Repetition of the phrase âsense of wonderâ is frequent. Varying terminology (e.g., âawe,â âcuriosityâ) would enhance stylistic engagement and precision.
This reference is over two decades old; incorporating more recent research into the Phineas Gage legacy would enhance the discussionâs relevance.
The Lord Kelvin versus Einstein contrast effectively exemplifies how premature certainty can stifle scientific wonder. This counterclaim deepens the natural sciences analysis.
Footnote formatting changes abruptly; standardizing citations throughout the essay will ensure academic consistency.
Inconsistent citation style in successive footnotes distracts the reader. Adopting a single referencing format strengthens scholarly presentation.
The concluding reflection on humility versus complacency introduces valuable ideas but could be strengthened by explicitly invoking TOK concepts like Ways of Knowing.

described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
